name: Seated Cat-Cow
description: A gentle spinal mobilization that relieves tension through the whole back.
steps: Sit upright at the edge of your seat with feet flat on the floor.
Inhale and arch your back, lifting your chest and drawing your shoulders back (Cow).
Exhale and round your spine, tucking your chin and drawing your belly in (Cat).
Repeat slowly 6–8 times, moving with your breath.
tips: Move at the speed of your breath — let the movement be fluid, not forced.

name: Seated Thoracic Rotation
description: Opens the mid-back and counters the forward hunch of desk posture.
steps: Sit tall and cross both arms over your chest.
Keeping your hips facing forward, slowly rotate your upper body to the right as far as comfortable.
Hold for 2 seconds, then rotate to the left.
Repeat 5 times on each side.

name: Seated Side Bend
description: Stretches the lateral muscles along the torso.
steps: Sit upright with feet flat on the floor.
Raise your right arm overhead and lean gently to the left.
Hold for 15 seconds, feeling the stretch along your right side.
Switch sides and repeat.

name: Seated Spinal Twist
description: A deep rotation that releases tension throughout the spine.
steps: Sit tall with feet flat on the floor.
Place your right hand on the outside of your left knee and your left hand on the back of the chair.
Inhale to lengthen your spine, then exhale and gently twist to the left.
Hold for 15 seconds then repeat on the other side.

name: Standing Back Extension
description: Counteracts spinal flexion from prolonged sitting.
steps: Stand up and place both hands on your lower back with fingers pointing down.
Gently lean backward, looking upward, feeling a stretch through the front of your torso.
Hold for 3 seconds, then return to upright.
Repeat 5 times.
tips: If you feel any pinching, reduce the range of motion.

name: Chest Opener
description: Opens the chest and front shoulders, countering forward rounding.
steps: Stand or sit tall.
Clasp your hands behind your back with arms straightened.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together and gently lift your clasped hands away from your body.
Hold for 20 seconds, breathing deeply.

name: Desk Forward Fold
description: Lengthens the entire spine using your desk for support.
steps: Stand facing your desk, feet hip-width apart.
Walk your feet back until your body forms an L-shape, hands resting on the desk surface.
Let your head drop between your arms and allow your spine to lengthen.
Hold for 30 seconds, breathing steadily.

name: Neck Side Tilt
description: Releases tension in the lateral neck and upper trapezius.
steps: Sit or stand tall, looking straight ahead.
Slowly tilt your right ear toward your right shoulder until you feel a gentle stretch on the left side of your neck.
Hold for 15 seconds.
Return to center and repeat on the left side.

name: Chin Tuck
description: Corrects forward head posture — the most common desk worker issue.
steps: Sit tall with shoulders relaxed.
Without moving your shoulders, gently draw your chin straight back as if making a double chin.
Hold for 5 seconds, then release.
Repeat 8 times.
tips: Keep your gaze level — don't tuck your chin down to your chest.

name: Neck Rotation
description: Restores range of motion in the cervical spine.
steps: Sit tall with shoulders relaxed.
Slowly turn your head to the right, looking over your shoulder as far as comfortable.
Hold for 5 seconds, then return to center.
Repeat on the left — 4 repetitions each side.

name: Upper Trapezius Stretch
description: Targets the upper trapezius, a common tension hotspot.
steps: Sit tall and reach your right hand under your chair, gripping the seat firmly.
Tilt your left ear toward your left shoulder.
For a deeper stretch, gently add light pressure with your left hand on the right side of your head.
Hold 20 seconds each side.
tips: Keep the gripping arm straight and shoulder pulled down throughout.

name: Levator Scapulae Stretch
description: Releases the muscle that often tightens from screen use and stress.
steps: Sit tall and place your right hand gently behind your head.
Look down toward your left knee, tucking your chin slightly.
Add gentle pressure with your right hand until you feel a stretch in the right side of your neck.
Hold 20 seconds, then switch sides.

name: Shoulder Rolls
description: Mobilizes and loosens the shoulder girdle.
steps: Sit or stand tall with arms loose at your sides.
Slowly roll both shoulders forward in large circles 5 times.
Reverse direction and roll backward 5 times.
Focus on moving through the full range of motion.

name: Scapular Squeeze
description: Activates the rhomboids and mid-traps to improve posture.
steps: Sit tall with arms at your sides and elbows bent to 90 degrees.
Gently squeeze your shoulder blades toward each other, as if holding a pencil between them.
Hold for 5 seconds, then release.
Repeat 10 times.
tips: Keep your shoulders away from your ears throughout.

name: Cross-Body Shoulder Stretch
description: Targets the posterior deltoid and rotator cuff.
steps: Bring your right arm across your body at shoulder height.
Use your left forearm to gently press the right arm closer to your chest.
Hold for 20 seconds, feeling the stretch in the back of your shoulder.
Switch arms and repeat.

name: Overhead Reach
description: Decompress the spine and stretch both sides of the torso.
steps: Sit or stand tall.
Raise both arms overhead and interlace your fingers, palms facing up.
Reach up and slightly to the right to stretch the left side.
Hold 10 seconds, then shift to the left side.

name: Doorframe Chest Stretch
description: Opens the pectorals and front shoulders that tighten from keyboard use.
steps: Stand beside your office wall or in a doorway.
Place your right forearm on the surface at shoulder height, elbow bent to 90 degrees.
Step your right foot forward until you feel a stretch across your right chest and front shoulder.
Hold 20 seconds and switch sides.

name: Wrist Circles
description: Lubricates the wrist joints and relieves stiffness from typing.
steps: Extend both arms in front of you, hands loosely fisted.
Rotate both wrists in large circles — 10 times clockwise.
Reverse direction — 10 times counterclockwise.
Let the movement be slow and through the full range.

name: Prayer Stretch
description: Stretches the wrist flexors, often overworked by typing and mouse use.
steps: Press both palms together in front of your chest, fingers pointing up.
Slowly lower your joined hands toward your waist while keeping palms pressed together.
Stop when you feel a stretch through your forearms and wrists.
Hold for 20 seconds.

name: Extensor Stretch
description: Targets the wrist extensors on the back of the forearm.
steps: Extend your right arm forward with palm facing down.
Use your left hand to gently press the fingers of your right hand downward and toward you.
Hold for 15 seconds, feeling the stretch along the top of your forearm.
Switch hands and repeat.

name: Finger Spreads
description: Mobilizes the small muscles of the hand.
steps: Hold both hands in front of you, fingers together.
Spread your fingers as wide apart as possible and hold for 3 seconds.
Make a loose fist and hold for 3 seconds.
Repeat 8 times.

name: Forearm Rotation
description: Restores supination/pronation range often limited by desk tasks.
steps: Hold both arms out in front of you, elbows at your sides, palms facing up.
Slowly rotate both forearms so your palms face down.
Rotate back to palms up.
Repeat 10 times slowly, feeling the motion through your forearms.

name: 20-20-20 Eye Break
description: The evidence-based rule for reducing digital eye strain.
steps: Look away from your screen and find a point at least 6 meters (20 feet) away.
Focus your gaze on that distant point for 20 seconds.
Let your eyes relax — don't force focus, just let them rest on the distance.
Blink naturally throughout.
tips: A window view works perfectly for this. The horizon is ideal.

name: Eye Palming
description: Rests the eyes in complete darkness to reduce fatigue.
steps: Rub your palms together briskly for 5 seconds to warm them.
Close your eyes and cup your warm palms gently over them without pressing on the eyeballs.
Breathe slowly and let your eyes rest in the darkness.
Hold for 30 seconds.

name: Focus Shifting
description: Exercises the ciliary muscles that control lens focusing.
steps: Hold your thumb up about 30 cm from your face.
Focus clearly on your thumb for 5 seconds.
Shift your focus to an object across the room for 5 seconds.
Alternate back and forth 5 times.

name: Eye Circles
description: Moves the eyes through their full range of motion.
steps: Close your eyes or look at a blank wall.
Without moving your head, slowly roll your eyes in a large clockwise circle.
Complete 5 full circles clockwise.
Reverse and complete 5 circles counterclockwise.

name: Conscious Blinking
description: Restores the tear film — screen use reduces blinking by up to 60%.
steps: Look slightly away from your screen or close your eyes.
Blink slowly and deliberately 10 times, fully closing your eyes each time.
On the last blink, squeeze your eyes gently shut for 3 seconds.
Open and look around the room naturally.

name: Seated Hip Flexor Stretch
description: Counters hip flexor tightening from prolonged sitting.
steps: Move to the edge of your seat with feet flat on the floor.
Slide your right foot backward so your right knee drops lower than your hip.
Sit tall and gently shift your weight forward until you feel a stretch in the front of your right hip.
Hold 20 seconds each side.
tips: Maintain an upright torso — don't lean forward at the waist.

name: Figure Four Hip Stretch
description: A seated pigeon pose that releases the glutes and outer hips.
steps: Sit tall and place your right ankle on your left knee, letting the right knee drop out to the side.
For a gentle stretch, simply hold this position upright.
For a deeper stretch, hinge slightly forward at the hips while keeping your back straight.
Hold 30 seconds each side.

name: Ankle Circles
description: Improves ankle mobility and encourages blood flow in the lower legs.
steps: Lift your right foot slightly off the floor.
Rotate your ankle in slow, large circles — 8 times in each direction.
Lower and switch to the left foot, repeating the same circles.

name: Standing Calf Raises
description: Activates the calf muscle pump to improve blood flow back to the heart.
steps: Stand behind your chair with hands resting on the back for balance.
Rise slowly onto your toes, lifting your heels as high as comfortable.
Hold at the top for 1 second, then slowly lower back down.
Repeat 12–15 times.

name: Standing Hip Circles
description: Loosens the hip joints and lumbar spine.
steps: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, hands on your hips.
Make large, slow circles with your hips — as if using a hula hoop.
Complete 5 circles in one direction.
Reverse and complete 5 circles the other way.

name: Hamstring Desk Stretch
description: Stretches the hamstrings using your desk for support.
steps: Stand and face your desk.
Extend your right leg straight and rest your heel on the edge of your desk or a chair seat.
Stand tall and hinge slightly forward from the hips until you feel a stretch behind your knee.
Hold 25 seconds each side.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the raised knee if this feels too intense.

name: Seated Leg Extensions
description: Activates the quadriceps and promotes circulation in the legs.
steps: Sit tall at the edge of your chair with feet flat on the floor.
Extend your right leg out straight, parallel to the floor, and hold for 3 seconds.
Lower it slowly back down.
Repeat 10 times on each leg.

name: Standing Calf Stretch
description: Releases calf tightness common from sitting with feet flat for hours.
steps: Stand facing your desk or wall, using it for balance.
Step your right foot back about 60 cm and press the heel firmly into the floor.
Lean your body gently forward, bending the front knee, until you feel a stretch in the right calf.
Hold 20 seconds each side.

name: Seated Glute Activation
description: Counters glute inhibition from prolonged sitting.
steps: Sit upright with feet flat on the floor.
Squeeze both glutes firmly, as if trying to hold a coin between them.
Hold for 5 seconds, then release completely.
Repeat 10 times.

name: Box Breathing
description: A proven technique for rapidly lowering cortisol and resetting focus.
steps: Exhale fully, then inhale slowly through your nose for a count of 4.
Hold your breath at the top for a count of 4.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for a count of 4.
Hold empty at the bottom for a count of 4. Repeat the cycle 4–6 times.
tips: Used by athletes and military personnel to manage acute stress.

name: Quick Body Scan
description: Brings you out of screen tunnel-vision and back into your body.
steps: Close your eyes or soften your gaze downward.
Starting from the top of your head, slowly bring attention to each part of your body.
Notice any tension or discomfort — just observe, don't try to fix it.
Move attention from head, to neck, shoulders, arms, chest, belly, hips, legs, and feet.

name: 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding
description: Engages all senses to rapidly reduce stress and restore presence.
steps: Name 5 things you can see right now.
Notice 4 things you can physically feel — the chair, your clothing, air temperature.
Listen for 3 distinct sounds in your environment.
Notice 2 things you can smell (or recall a favorite scent), and 1 thing you can taste.

name: Breath Counting
description: A simple mindfulness practice to quiet mental chatter.
steps: Sit comfortably and close your eyes.
Take a natural breath in, and as you exhale, silently count '1'.
Continue naturally — count '2' on the next exhale, up to '10'.
If your mind wanders or you lose count, gently start again from '1' without judgment.
